title: Hydraulic Power Steering Fluid Flush and Bleed
description: Flushes and bleeds hydraulic power steering systems after hose, pump, or rack work. Not applicable to EPS-only vehicles.
physical_solution: Drain reservoir, refill with correct fluid, then bleed by turning lock-to-lock with front wheels off ground. Repeat until no bubbles and noise is gone.

technical_solution.elm327_feasibility: Limited — no scan commands required for hydraulic bleed. ELM327 not needed.

procedure_steps.description: Confirm vehicle uses hydraulic PS. If EPS (electric rack), stop — this procedure does not apply.
Drain reservoir and return line. Reconnect lines and fill with correct fluid to MAX.
Engine OFF: turn steering wheel lock-to-lock 10 times slowly. Check fluid level and top off.
Start engine and idle. Turn lock-to-lock 5 more times. Do not hold at full lock more than 2 seconds.
Inspect reservoir for foam/bubbles. If present, wait 5 minutes and repeat bleed cycle.
Road test and verify no whining, no heavy steering, and no leaks at hose/rack connections.

oem_notes.Toyota/Lexus: Older Toyota/Lexus typically use ATF Dexron III equivalent in hydraulic PS systems. Many 2012+ switched to EPS.
oem_notes.Honda/Acura: Use Honda Power Steering Fluid only (not ATF). Older Accord/CR-V use hydraulic systems.
oem_notes.Nissan/Infiniti: Most older trucks/SUVs use ATF-based PS fluid. Frontier/Xterra commonly hydraulic.
oem_notes.Ford: Older F-150 and body-on-frame platforms hydraulic. Use Mercon V/LV per model spec.
oem_notes.GM: Trucks/SUVs commonly hydraulic through mid-2010s. Use GM approved PS fluid/ATF spec by model year.
oem_notes.BMW: Older BMW may use CHF-11S/CHF-202 (NOT ATF). Check reservoir cap spec.
oem_notes.VW/Audi: Hydraulic systems use Pentosin CHF variants on older models. Many newer MQB platforms are EPS.
oem_notes.Stellantis: Many Ram/Jeep older platforms hydraulic; use ATF+4 on many models.
oem_notes.Hyundai/Kia: Mostly EPS on modern platforms; older hydraulic systems use OEM PS fluid spec.
